1: vaashuv vaessa et einai vaere vehineh arba markavot yotz'ot min bein shene heharim veheharim harim nechoshet: 'Again I lifted my eyes and saw, and behold, four chariots came out from between two mountains. And the mountains were mountains of bronze.' The eighth and final vision: the four chariots. Arba markavot (four chariots) — the divine war-chariots corresponding to the four patrol riders of the first vision (1:8-11). The two mountains of bronze (harim nechoshet) suggest either the twin mountains flanking Jerusalem (Moriah and the Mount of Olives) or the celestial gateposts of the divine realm, made of incorruptible bronze. The chariots emerge from YHWH's presence to execute his will on the earth.
2: bamarkavah harishonah susim adumim uvmarkavah hashenit susim shechorim: 'The first chariot had red horses, the second black horses.' The four chariot-teams: red, black, white, and dappled (vv2-3). The color-associations: red = war/bloodshed; black = famine/death (cf. Rev 6:2-8, the four horsemen of the Apocalypse echo Zechariah's four chariots). The Revelation vision of the four horsemen (Rev 6:1-8) is the NT's most direct development of Zechariah's four-chariot vision.
3: uvmarkavah hashlishit susim levanim uvmarkavah har'vit susim beruddim amutzim: 'The third white horses, the fourth chariot dappled horses — all of them powerful.' Beruddim amutzim (dappled, powerful/strong) — the dappled horses sent to the south (v6). The four chariot colors correspond to the four compass directions: north (black), south (dappled), west (white, going after them), east (red, kept to go over the whole earth). YHWH's executive agents cover all four directions of the earth.
4: va'an veomer el hamalakh haddover bi mah eleh Adonai: 'Then I answered and said to the angel who talked with me, What are these, my lord?' The standard interpretive question. The angel's response (v5) identifies the chariots as the four winds/spirits of heaven — YHWH's executive agents sent to patrol and act throughout all the earth.
5: vaya'an hamalakh vayomer elai eleh arba ruchot hashamayim yotz'ot mithatzeiv al Adon kol ha'aretz: 'And the angel answered and said to me, These are the four winds of heaven, going out after presenting themselves before the Lord of all the earth.' The four winds (arba ruchot hashamayim) = the four spirits/winds of heaven, YHWH's executive agents dispatched from the divine council. They stand before the Lord of the whole earth (Adon kol ha'aretz) before deployment — receiving their commissions at the divine court before going to execute YHWH's purposes in history.
6: asher bah susim hashchorim yotz'im el eretz tzafon vehalevavim yatze'u el achar'eihem vehaveruddim yatze'u el eretz hateman: 'The one with the black horses goes toward the north country, the white ones go after them, and the dappled ones go toward the south country.' The deployment: black (north = Babylon/Assyria), white (west/after the black), dappled (south = Egypt). The dominant direction is north — Babylon, the primary object of divine attention in post-exilic prophecy.
7: vaha'amutzim yatze'u vevakeshu lalechet lehithalek ba'aretz vayomer lekhu hithalku ba'aretz vayithalku ba'aretz: 'When the powerful horses came out, they were impatient to go and patrol the earth. And he said, Go, patrol the earth. So they patrolled the earth.' The powerful (strong, dappled) horses are eager to go — restless for action. The divine commission is simple: Go, patrol the earth. The response: they go. YHWH's executive agents are obedient, powerful, and comprehensive in their coverage. The frame of the book: first vision (ch1 — patrol returns with report of rest) and final vision (ch6 — patrol commissioned to go) form a bracket around the eight visions.
8: vayizaq oti vayedabber elai leimor re'eh hayotze'im el eretz tzafon heniichu et ruchi be'eretz tzafon: 'Then he cried to me, Behold, those who go toward the north country have set my Spirit at rest in the north country.' The black horses going to the north ('quieted' or 'set YHWH's Spirit at rest' in the north) means the divine wrath against Babylon has been satisfied — YHWH's purpose accomplished there. The Babylonian empire was destroyed in 539 BCE (Cyrus); by Zechariah's time (520-518 BCE) YHWH's judgment of Babylon is complete. The first vision (ch1) showed the earth at rest while Jerusalem suffered; this final vision shows YHWH's wrath against the north satisfied, and restoration about to begin.
9: vayehi davar YHWH elai leimor: 'The word of the LORD came to me: Receive from the exiles...' This oracle (vv9-15) is the symbolic coronation of Joshua the high priest with a crown, accompanied by the messianic oracle of the Branch (v12-13). The reception of silver and gold from the returning exiles (Heldai, Tobijah, Jedaiah, who had come from Babylon) for making the crown connects the material gifts of the diaspora to the symbolic act of crowning.
10: qach me'et hagolah me'et Cheldai ume'et Toviyah ume'et Yedayah uba'ta ata bayom hahu uba'ta beit Yoshiyah ben Tzefanyah: 'Take silver and gold from the exiles — from Heldai, Tobijah, and Jedaiah — who have arrived from Babylon, and go the same day to the house of Josiah, the son of Zephaniah.' The three donors from Babylon bring materials for the crown. Their arrival from exile on this specific day is providential: they become the human instruments of the symbolic act. The house of Josiah son of Zephaniah is the staging point for the crown-making.
11: velaqachta kesef vezahav ve'asita atarot venasata berosh Yehoshua ben Yehotzadak hakohen hagadol: 'Take from them silver and gold, and make a crown, and set it on the head of Joshua, the son of Jehozadak, the high priest.' The crowning of Joshua (atarot, H5850 — crown, plural of majesty) on the head of the high priest is a symbolic action. Note: the crown is placed on the head of Joshua the priest, not Zerubbabel the prince — because the oracle (v12-13) is about the Branch, the coming messianic figure, who will be both priest and king. Joshua acts as the symbolic representative/type of the coming priest-king.
12: ve'amarta elav leimor koh amar YHWH tzvaot leimor hineh ish Tsemach shmo umitachtav yitzmach uvanah et heikhal YHWH: 'And say to him, Thus says the LORD of hosts: Behold, the man whose name is the Branch — he will branch out from his place and he will build the temple of the LORD.' The messianic announcement of the vision: ish Tsemach shmo (a man whose name is the Branch). Tsemach (H6780 — a shoot, a sprout, a branch) is the primary royal-messianic title in Zechariah (cf. 3:8) and Jeremiah (23:5; 33:15: I will raise up for David a righteous Branch). The wordplay on yitzmach (he will branch/sprout from his place) is intentional: the Branch branches out — his nature is expressed in his name. He will build the temple — in the ultimate sense, Christ is the builder of the spiritual temple (Matt 16:18; Eph 2:20-22).
13: vehu yivneh et heikhal YHWH vehu yissa hod veyashav umashol al kis'o vehayah kohen al kis'o va'atzat shalom tihyeh bein shenehem: 'It is he who shall build the temple of the LORD and shall bear royal honor, and shall sit and rule on his throne. And there shall be a priest on his throne, and the counsel of peace shall be between them both.' The single greatest Christological verse in Zechariah: the Branch will be simultaneously king (sit and rule on his throne) and priest (kohen on his throne). The priest-king synthesis — the combining of two offices that were scrupulously separated in the OT (Uzziah was struck with leprosy for attempting it, 2 Chr 26:16-21) — is only possible in the messianic figure. Hebrews 7 identifies Christ as the fulfillment: a priest forever in the order of Melchizedek (who was both king of Salem and priest, Gen 14:18), bearing the glory of both offices in one person.
14: veha'atarah tihyeh leCheldai uleToviahu uleYedayah veleChen ben Tzefanyah lezikkaron beheikhal YHWH: 'And the crown shall be in the temple of the LORD as a reminder to Helem, Tobijah, Jedaiah, and Hen the son of Zephaniah.' The crown is deposited in the temple as a memorial (zikkaron, H2146 — a memorial, a reminder, a sign). The crown that was placed on Joshua's head now becomes a permanent symbolic object in the temple, pointing forward to the coming priest-king who will wear the royal and priestly crowns permanently. The memorial function: the symbolic crowning of Joshua points forward to the Messiah's actual enthronement.
15: urchoqim yavo'u uvanah beheikhal YHWH vidatem ki YHWH tzvaot shelachani aleikhem vehayah im shamo'a tishme'u bekol YHWH Eloheikhem: 'And those who are far off shall come and help to build the temple of the LORD. And you shall know that the LORD of hosts has sent me to you. And this shall come to pass, if you will diligently obey the voice of the LORD your God.' The expansion: 'those who are far off' (rechogim) — the distant exiles and, by extension, the Gentiles — will come to build the temple. The NT fulfillment: Gentiles incorporated into the spiritual temple (Eph 2:19-22: built together into a dwelling place for God by the Spirit). The closing condition ('if you will diligently obey') maintains the covenant's conditional character even within an eschatological promise.
